summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the rule for naming an acid from a polyatomic ion ending in '-ate'?

When a polyatomic ion ends in '-ate,' you change the ending to '-ic acid' and use the element root. For example, sulfate becomes sulfuric acid, and nitrate becomes nitric acid.

What is the rule for naming an acid from a polyatomic ion ending in '-ite'?

When a polyatomic ion ends in '-ite,' you change the ending to '-ous acid' and use the element root. For example, sulfite becomes sulfurous acid, and nitrite becomes nitrous acid.

What is the rule for naming a binary acid (no oxygen)?

Binary acids (containing only hydrogen and one other nonmetal) are named with the pattern: hydro- + element root + -ic acid. For example, HCl is hydrochloric acid and HBr is hydrobromic acid.

What is the difference between sulfuric acid and sulfurous acid?

Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) comes from sulfate (SO₄²⁻, ending in '-ate' gives '-ic acid'). Sulfurous acid (H₂SO₃) comes from sulfite (SO₃²⁻, ending in '-ite' gives '-ous acid'). They differ by one oxygen atom.

What is the naming convention for the acid formed from the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻)?

Carbonate (CO₃²⁻) ends in '-ate,' so the acid uses the '-ic acid' suffix: carbonic acid (H₂CO₃). The 'hydro-' prefix is only used for binary acids, not oxyacids.

What is the memory aid 'I ate something icky' used for in acid naming?

The phrase 'I ate something icky' helps students remember that polyatomic ions ending in '-ate' form acids ending in '-ic.' Similarly, '-ite' ions form '-ous acid' names.

A student is given the formula HClO₄ and told it comes from the perchlorate ion (ClO₄⁻). What is the correct acid name?

Perchlorate (ClO₄⁻) ends in '-ate,' so the acid name uses '-ic acid.' The 'per-' prefix is kept, giving perchloric acid. The 'per-' prefix indicates one more oxygen than the '-ate' form.

A student needs to name the acid H₃PO₃, which comes from the phosphite ion (PO₃³⁻). What is the correct name?

Phosphite (PO₃³⁻) ends in '-ite,' so the acid name uses '-ous acid': phosphorous acid (H₃PO₃). Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) comes from phosphate (PO₄³⁻), which ends in '-ate.'

A student is working on a naming worksheet and encounters the acid HBrO₃. She knows it comes from the bromate ion (BrO₃⁻). What is the correct name?

Bromate (BrO₃⁻) ends in '-ate,' so the acid name uses '-ic acid': bromic acid. Hydrobromic acid is the binary acid HBr, and bromous acid would come from bromite (BrO₂⁻).

A student is given an unknown acid in a lab and determines its formula is HClO. She knows hypochlorite (ClO⁻) ends in '-ite'. What is the acid name?

Hypochlorite (ClO⁻) ends in '-ite,' so the acid uses '-ous acid.' The 'hypo-' prefix is kept from the ion name, giving hypochlorous acid (HClO). This is the acid found in bleach solutions.
